Muhammad Imran) Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 (Muhammad Imran Vs. The State.) Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 (Mst. Sajida Bibi Vs. The State.) J U D G M E N T Date of hearing: 23.02.2022. Appellants by: Syed Zeeshan Haider, Advocate for Muhammad Imran (the appellant in Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019). Ms. Tania Bazai, Advocate (learned defence counsel appointed on State expense to represent Mst. Sajida Bibi, the appellant in Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019). State by: Ch. Asghar Ali Gill, Deputy Prosecutor General. Complainant by: Mirza Muhammad Azam, Advocate. SADIQ MAHMUD KHURRAM, J.–Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam and Mst. Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad (convicts) were tried by the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Bahawalnagar in the case FIR No. 262 of 2018 dated 07.08.2018 registered in respect of offences under sections 302 and 34 PPC at Police Station City A-Division, Bahawalnagar, District Bahawalnagar for committing the Qatl-i-Amd of Maqbool Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased). The learned trial court vide judgment dated 2 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 29.01.2019, convicted Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam and Mst. Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad (convicts) and sentenced them as infra: Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam:- Death under section 302(b) PPC as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Maqbool Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.250,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of the deceased and in case of default thereof, the convict was directed to further undergo six months of simple imprisonment.

The learned trial court vide judgment dated 2 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 29.01.2019, convicted Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam and Mst. Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad (convicts) and sentenced them as infra: Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam:- Death under section 302(b) PPC as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Maqbool Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.250,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of the deceased and in case of default thereof, the convict was directed to further undergo six months of simple imprisonment. The convict was ordered to be hanged by his neck till dead. Mst. Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad:- Imprisonment for life under section 302(b) PPC as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Maqbool Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased). Mst. Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad (convict) was, however, extended the benefit available under Section 382-B of Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 2. Feeling aggrieved, Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam (convict) lodged Criminal Appeal No.59-J of 2019 through jail assailing his conviction and sentence. Feeling aggrieved, Mst. Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad (convict) lodged Criminal Appeal No.61-J of 2019 through jail assailing her conviction and sentence. The learned trial court submitted Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 under section 374 Cr.P.C. seeking confirmation or otherwise of the sentence of death awarded to the appellant namely Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam. We intend to dispose of the Criminal Appeal No.59-J of 2019, Criminal Appeal No.61-J of 2019 and Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 through this single judgment. 3. Precisely, the necessary facts of the prosecution case, as narrated by Asad Mahmood (PW-3), the complainant of the case, are as under:- 3 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 “On 07-08-2018 at about 5:45 AM I along with my brother Abdul Ghafoor and my brother-in-law Muhammad Afzal P.Ws was sitting on the roof of my house. We were discussing about some business and we heard hue and cry from the house of my brother Maqbool Ahmed deceased of this case. We rushed to the house of Maqbool Ahmed and saw that Sajida Bibi had caught hold by hands of my bool and Imran was having with pistol.

We were discussing about some business and we heard hue and cry from the house of my brother Maqbool Ahmed deceased of this case. We rushed to the house of Maqbool Ahmed and saw that Sajida Bibi had caught hold by hands of my bool and Imran was having with pistol. Sajida Bibi shouted and asked Imran (مقبول احمد کو راستے سے ہٹا دو) on which Imran made straight fire to my brother Maqbool which landed on his chest. Maqbool fell down on the ground. We apprehended accused persons present in the court, at spot. Accused resisted but we over powered both of them. We attended Maqbool and due to his precarious condition we called 1122 through my mobile phone. The 1122 rescue team reached at the spot. They rescued Maqbool to hospital. After sometime police came to the spot. I got drafted application Exh.PA about the occurrence and submitted to the police officer. I had signed beneath my application Exh.PA. Maqbool died later on. Motive behind the occurrence was the illicit relations of Imran and Sajida Bibi present in the court.” 4. After the formal investigation of the case report under section 173 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 was submitted before the learned trial court and the accused were sent to face trial. The learned trial court framed the charge against the accused on 22.09.2018, to which the accused pleaded not guilty and claimed trial. 5. The prosecution in order to prove its case got statements of as many as twelve witnesses recorded. The ocular account of the case was furnished by Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4). Muhammad Saleem, SI (PW-1) stated that on 07.08.2018, he recorded the formal F.I.R (Exh.PA/1). Muhammad Taj, ASI (PW-2) stated that on 07.08.2018, he went to the place of occurrence and received the written application (Exh. PA) of Asad Mahmood (PW-3). Hassan Javed 1586/C (PW-5) stated that on 07.08.2018, 4 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 he got conducted the post mortem examination of the dead body of the deceased and also received the last worn clothes of the deceased from the Medical Officer and the Investigating Officer of the case took the photographs (P-3/1-3) of the dead body of the deceased in his presence.

Hassan Javed 1586/C (PW-5) stated that on 07.08.2018, 4 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 he got conducted the post mortem examination of the dead body of the deceased and also received the last worn clothes of the deceased from the Medical Officer and the Investigating Officer of the case took the photographs (P-3/1-3) of the dead body of the deceased in his presence. Muhammad Din Abid, Map Planner (PW-10), prepared the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh. PJ). Manzoor Ahmad 681/HC (PW-12) stated that on 07.08.2018, he received one sealed parcel said to contain blood stained earth, one sealed parcel said to contain a pistol and six sealed parcels said to contain items to be deposited to the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore and on 12.08.2018, he handed over all the sealed parcels to Shahid Ayyub, ASI (PW-6) for their onward transmission to the office of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore. Shafaqat Ali, SI (PW-11), investigated the case from 07.08.2018 till 15.09.2018, arrested the appellants on 07.08.2018, and detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. 6. The prosecution also got Dr. Mobeen Ahmad (PW-7) examined, who on 07.08.2018 was posted as Medical Officer at DHQ hospital Bahawalnagar and on the same day conducted the postmortem examination of the dead body of Maqbool Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased). Dr. Mobeen Ahmad (PW- 7), on examining the dead body of Maqbool Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased) observed as under:- “According to record, death was caused on 07-08-2018 at 7:30 AM, dead body was received in dead house on 07-08-2018 at about 7:45 AM and police documents were received on 07-08-2018 at 2:20 PM. I conducted the postmortem examination on 07-08-2018 at 2:30 PM.............. 5 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 INJURIES. 1) An oval shape lacerated wound on front of chest near medial one third of left clavicle lead staining burning present, margins were inverted, direction of the wound was backward and downward, measuring 1 cm x 0.75 cm in, it was an entry wound. 2) A lacerated wound on back of right side middle of lumber region, margins were everted, direction was towards injury No.1 measuring 2 cm x 1.5 cm, it was an exit wound..............

Mobeen Ahmad (PW- 7), on examining the dead body of Maqbool Ahmad son of Rehmat Ali (deceased) observed as under:- “According to record, death was caused on 07-08-2018 at 7:30 AM, dead body was received in dead house on 07-08-2018 at about 7:45 AM and police documents were received on 07-08-2018 at 2:20 PM. I conducted the postmortem examination on 07-08-2018 at 2:30 PM.............. 5 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 INJURIES. 1) An oval shape lacerated wound on front of chest near medial one third of left clavicle lead staining burning present, margins were inverted, direction of the wound was backward and downward, measuring 1 cm x 0.75 cm in, it was an entry wound. 2) A lacerated wound on back of right side middle of lumber region, margins were everted, direction was towards injury No.1 measuring 2 cm x 1.5 cm, it was an exit wound.............. OPINION. In my opinion, the cause of death in this case was due to injuries No.1 and 2 which caused damage to main blood vessels of chest right lung, liver and main blood vessels of abdomen leading to severe haemorrhage, shock and death. These injuries were ante mortem and caused by firearm weapon and were sufficient to cause death in ordinary course of nature.” The prosecution also got Dr. Yasmeen Kamboh (PW-8) examined, who on 07.08.2018, was posted as Woman Medical Officer at DHQ hospital Bahawalnagar and on the same day medically examined Mst. Sajida Perveen wife of Maqbool Ahmad (the appellant in Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019) and issued the Medico Legal Examination Certificate No. 759 dated 07.08.2018 (Exh. PG). The prosecution also got Dr. Mobeen Ahmad (PW-9) examined who remained posted with and was acquainted with the handwriting Dr. Aftab Saleem as Dr. Aftab Saleem was not available and his attendance could not be procured without an amount of delay which, under the circumstances of the case, the learned trial court considered unreasonable. Dr. Mobeen Ahmad (PW-9) stated that on 07.08.2018 Dr. Aftab Saleem was posted as Medical Officer at the DHQ hospital, Bahawalnagar and on the same day medically examined the appellant namely Muhammad Imran and issued the Medico Legal Examination Certificate (Exh. PH). 6 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 7. On 21.01.2019, the learned Deputy District Public Prosecutor tendered in evidence the reports of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh.

On 06.11.2018, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor gave up the prosecution witness namely Muhammad Asghar 590/C as being unnecessary. On 21.01.2019, the learned Deputy District Public Prosecutor tendered in evidence the reports of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh. PL, Exh. PM and Exh. PN). On 22.01.2019, the learned Deputy District Public Prosecutor closed the prosecution evidence after tendering in evidence the reports of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh. PP and Exh. PQ). 8. After the closure of prosecution evidence, the learned trial court examined the appellants namely Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam and Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad under section 342 Cr.P.C. and in answer to the question why this case against you and why the PWs have deposed against you, they replied that they had been involved in the case falsely and were innocent. The appellants namely Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam and Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad opted not to get themselves examined under section 340 Cr.P.C. and did not adduce any evidence in their defence. 9. At the conclusion of the trial, the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Bahawalnagar convicted and sentenced the appellants as referred to above. 10. The contention of the learned counsels for the appellants precisely is that the whole case is fabricated and false and the prosecution remained unable to prove the facts in issue and did not produce any unimpeachable, admissible, 7 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 and relevant evidence. Learned counsels for the appellants further contended that the story of the prosecution mentioned in the statements of the witnesses, on the face of it, was highly improbable. Learned counsels for the appellants further contended that the statements of the prosecution witnesses were not worthy of any reliance. The learned counsels for the appellants also submitted that the recoveries made in the case were full of procedural defects, of no legal worth and value, and were the result of fake proceedings.

Learned counsels for the appellants further contended that the statements of the prosecution witnesses were not worthy of any reliance. The learned counsels for the appellants also submitted that the recoveries made in the case were full of procedural defects, of no legal worth and value, and were the result of fake proceedings. The learned counsels for the appellants also argued that the appellants had been involved in the occurrence only on suspicion. The learned counsels for the appellants finally submitted that the prosecution has totally failed to prove the case against the accused beyond the shadow of a doubt. 11. On the other hand, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel appearing on behalf of the complainant contended that the prosecution has proved its case beyond the shadow of doubt by producing independent witnesses. They further argued that the deceased died as a result of injuries suffered at the hands of the appellant namely Muhammad Imran. They further contended that the medical evidence also corroborated the statements of the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4). They further argued that the recovery of the pistol (P-5) also corroborated the ocular account. They further contended that there was no occasion for the prosecution witnesses, who were related to the deceased, to substitute the real offenders with the innocent in this case. Lastly, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General and the learned counsel appearing on behalf of the complainant prayed for the rejection of the appeals as lodged by 8 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 the appellants namely Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam and Mst. Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad. 12. We have heard the learned counsels for the appellants, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General, the learned counsel appearing on behalf of the complainant and with their able assistance perused the record and evidence recorded during the trial. 13.

Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad. 12. We have heard the learned counsels for the appellants, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General, the learned counsel appearing on behalf of the complainant and with their able assistance perused the record and evidence recorded during the trial. 13. According to the evidence produced by the prosecution during the course of the trial, the occurrence was witnessed by the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4). Both the said prosecution witnesses were not only related to each other but also with the deceased namely Maqbool Ahmad. The deceased namely Maqbool Ahmad was the real brother of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW- 3) and the brother-in-law (brother of wife) of the prosecution witness namely Muhammad Afzal (PW-4). It was further brought on record that the occurrence took place inside the house of the deceased namely, Maqbool Ahmad, whereas both the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) had their own separate houses. The prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) admitted in his statement before the learned trial court that before the occurrence, he was present on the roof of his own house whereas the occurrence took place inside the Television viewing room of the house of the deceased. Asad Mahmood (PW-3) during cross-examination stated as under:- 9 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 “ The house of occurrence consisted of about 3/ 3⁄4 Marla of land but it was not transferred in the name of Maqbool Ahmed deceased. Deceased was residing in the house of occurrence for the last four five years. He had constructed this house i.e. the place of occurrence............ My house is adjoining to the house of occurrence towards north. The intervening wall of our houses is about 11 feet height. The opening doors of the house of occurrence and my house falls towards east.................

The intervening wall of our houses is about 11 feet height. The opening doors of the house of occurrence and my house falls towards east................. The place of occurrence is TV lounge of the house of occurrence.”(emphasis supplied) It was also admitted by the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW- 3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) that the house of the other prosecution witness namely Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) was in another locality which was at a distance of three kilometres from the other house of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3). Asad Mahmood (PW-3) during cross- examination admitted as under:- “ It is correct that my brother Abdul Ghafoor PW resides in Railway colony Bahawalnagar at a distance about 1 1⁄2 K.M and Muhammad Afzal PW resides in Taqwa Colony Bahawalnagar at about 3 K.M from my house.” As both the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) were admittedly not the residents of the house in the room of which the occurrence had taken place and that too early in the morning at 5.45 a.m., the presence of the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4), at the place of occurrence, could not be termed as in the normal course of events, therefore, the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal 10 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 (PW-4) can be validly termed as “chance witnesses” and therefore were under a bounden duty to provide a convincing reason for arrival presence at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence and were also under a duty to prove their presence by producing some physical proof of the same. We have noted with grave concern that the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) failed miserably to provide any consistent evidence as to their arrival at the place of occurrence and that too at the very moment when the same was taking place.

Asad Mahmood (PW-3) during cross- examination admitted as under:- “ It is correct that my brother Abdul Ghafoor PW resides in Railway colony Bahawalnagar at a distance about 1 1⁄2 K.M and Muhammad Afzal PW resides in Taqwa Colony Bahawalnagar at about 3 K.M from my house.” As both the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) were admittedly not the residents of the house in the room of which the occurrence had taken place and that too early in the morning at 5.45 a.m., the presence of the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4), at the place of occurrence, could not be termed as in the normal course of events, therefore, the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal 10 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 (PW-4) can be validly termed as “chance witnesses” and therefore were under a bounden duty to provide a convincing reason for arrival presence at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence and were also under a duty to prove their presence by producing some physical proof of the same. We have noted with grave concern that the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) failed miserably to provide any consistent evidence as to their arrival at the place of occurrence and that too at the very moment when the same was taking place. According to the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4), they were sitting on the roof of the house of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) when they heard the hue and cry coming out of the house of the deceased and subsequently they rushed there and saw the occurrence. During the course of the investigation, the Investigating Officers of the case remained present inside the house of the deceased where the occurrence had taken place for a considerable time and for long hours, however, the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) did not produce any evidence to prove that they were sitting on the roof of the house of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) just before the occurrence took place. During the course of their stay at the place of occurrence, neither Muhammad Taj, ASI (PW-2), the police officer who received the written application (Exh. PA) of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) nor Shafaqat Ali, SI (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, observed any chairs or cots or anything else on the roof of the house of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3), which could prove that the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and 11 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) were indeed present on the said roof half an hour before 5.45 a.m. from where they subsequently reached at the place of the occurrence, along with the other recoveries, though there was no occasion for the said articles not to have been present on the roof of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) or not being taken into possession by the Investigating Officers during their visits and stay at the place of the occurrence, if the same were available.

During the course of their stay at the place of occurrence, neither Muhammad Taj, ASI (PW-2), the police officer who received the written application (Exh. PA) of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) nor Shafaqat Ali, SI (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, observed any chairs or cots or anything else on the roof of the house of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3), which could prove that the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and 11 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) were indeed present on the said roof half an hour before 5.45 a.m. from where they subsequently reached at the place of the occurrence, along with the other recoveries, though there was no occasion for the said articles not to have been present on the roof of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) or not being taken into possession by the Investigating Officers during their visits and stay at the place of the occurrence, if the same were available. It only proves that there did not exist any physical evidence proving the presence of the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) on the roof of the house of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) prior to the occurrence from where they subsequently rushed to the place of occurrence. We have also perused the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh. PJ) prepared by Muhammad Din Abid, Map Planner (PW-10) and the rough site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh. PK), prepared by Shafaqat Ali, SI (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, and no place where the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) were present prior to the occurrence has been marked in the said site plans (Exh. PJ and Exh. PK) despite the fact that the house of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) has been mentioned in the said site plans. Muhammad Din Abid, Map Planner (PW-10) admitted during cross- examination that while preparing the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh. PJ), he had not visited the roof of the house where the occurrence took place. The prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) had claimed that they rushed to the place of occurrence after hearing the hue and cry coming from inside the 12 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 house of the deceased while they were sitting on the roof of the house of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3). As mentioned above, according to the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3), there was a wall, as high as eleven feet, between the houses of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and the deceased.

The prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) had claimed that they rushed to the place of occurrence after hearing the hue and cry coming from inside the 12 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 house of the deceased while they were sitting on the roof of the house of the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3). As mentioned above, according to the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3), there was a wall, as high as eleven feet, between the houses of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and the deceased. The scaling over the said wall by the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) was a feat easier said than done. During the course of cross-examination, the prosecution witness namely Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) claimed that they had scaled over the said wall and then rushed down the stairs to arrive at the place of occurrence, whereas the prosecution witness namely Asad Mahmood (PW- 3) had already stated that the height of the said wall to be 11 feet. Asad Mahmood (PW-3) during cross-examination stated as under:- “The intervening wall of our houses is about 11 feet height (sic).”(emphasis supplied) Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) during cross-examination claimed as under:- “Just after hearing the noise from the house of occurrence we after scaling over the intervening wall on the roofs rushed to the place of occurrence through stairs. ”(emphasis supplied) It has not been explained by the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) as to how they were able to scale over the wall having such substantial height. Moreover, Shafaqat Ali, (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, during cross-examination, admitted that none of the witnesses who were present at the place of occurrence stated before him that they had scaled over the wall between the 13 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 houses of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and the deceased and thereafter taken the stairs down from the roof of the house of the deceased to arrive at the place of occurrence. Shafaqat Ali, (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, during cross-examination stated as under:- “ None of the witnesses stated before me that from the roof they reached in the room i.e Place of occurrence from stairs of the house.” In this manner, the eye witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) failed miserably to establish their presence together on the roof of the house of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) half an hour prior to 5.45 a.m. in the morning and their subsequent arrival at the place of occurrence.

Moreover, Shafaqat Ali, (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, during cross-examination, admitted that none of the witnesses who were present at the place of occurrence stated before him that they had scaled over the wall between the 13 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 houses of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and the deceased and thereafter taken the stairs down from the roof of the house of the deceased to arrive at the place of occurrence. Shafaqat Ali, (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, during cross-examination stated as under:- “ None of the witnesses stated before me that from the roof they reached in the room i.e Place of occurrence from stairs of the house.” In this manner, the eye witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) failed miserably to establish their presence together on the roof of the house of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) half an hour prior to 5.45 a.m. in the morning and their subsequent arrival at the place of occurrence. The prosecution was under a bounden duty to establish that the occurrence had indeed taken place when the prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4), while hearing the hue and cry coming from the inside of the house of the deceased, had proceeded to the same and arrived at the place of the occurrence but the failure to prove their presence on the roof of the house of Asad Mahmood (PW-3) half an hour before before 5.45 a.m., has vitiated our trust in Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4). 14. We have noted that according to the witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4), they were in clear view, at a meagre distance, from the assailant and unarmed whereas the appellant namely Muhammad Imran was allegedly armed with a pistol. The prosecution witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) also claimed to had detained both the appellants at the place of occurrence, after 14 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 the occurrence. Asad Mahmood (PW-3) during cross-examination claimed that he had also beaten the appellant namely Muhammad Imran and stated during cross-examination as under:- “ I had slapped and gave fist blows to accused at that time when we apprehended them.” Had the witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) been present in the view of the assailant, then they would not have been spared. Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) even did not claim that they did not receive any injury because they had hidden or were not visible to the assailant.

Asad Mahmood (PW-3) during cross-examination claimed that he had also beaten the appellant namely Muhammad Imran and stated during cross-examination as under:- “ I had slapped and gave fist blows to accused at that time when we apprehended them.” Had the witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) been present in the view of the assailant, then they would not have been spared. Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) even did not claim that they did not receive any injury because they had hidden or were not visible to the assailant. Blessing Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) with such an incredible consideration and showing them such favour is implausible and opposed to the natural behaviour of any accused. It is all the more illogical that being perceptive of the fact that if the witnesses were left alive, they would depose against the accused, even then the appellant namely Muhammad Imran, who allegedly was armed with a pistol and had only fired once with the same, did not cause any injury to them. According to the report of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh. PM), the magazine of the Pistol had three live rounds still loaded into it when the sdame was examined by the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore. Neither there was any dearth of ammunition nor that of intent and opportunity on part of the appellant namely Muhammad Imran for not doing away with the witnesses namely Asad Mahmood (PW-3) and Muhammad Afzal (PW-4) or to make any effort to escape even. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General submits that it was in the knowledge of the appellant how the deceased died so it was the appellant who was responsible, in absence of any explanation. The law on the burden of proof, as provided in Article 117 of the Qanune-Shahadat, 1984, mandates the prosecution to prove, and that too, beyond any doubt, the guilt of the accused for the commission of the crime for which he is charged. The said provision provides: “117. Burden of proof:- Whoever desires any Court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts, must prove that those facts exist. When a person is bound to prove the existence of any fact, it is said that the burden of proof lies on that person.” On a conceptual plain, Article 117 of the Qanune-Shahadat, 1984 enshrines the foundational principle of our criminal justice system, whereby the accused is presumed to be innocent unless proved otherwise. Accordingly, the burden is placed on the prosecution to prove beyond doubt the guilt of the accused which burden can never be shifted to the accused, unless the legislature by express terms commands otherwise. It is only when the prosecution is able to discharge the burden of proof by establishing the elements of the offence, which are sufficient to bring home the guilt of the accused then, the burden is shifted upon the accused, inter alia, under Article 122 of the Qanune- Shahadat, 1984, to produce evidence of facts, which are especially in his 34 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 exclusive knowledge, and practically impossible for the prosecution to prove, to avoid conviction. Article 122 reads as under: “122. Burden of proving fact especially within knowledge:- When any fact is especially within the knowledge of any person, the burden to proving that fact is upon him.” It has to be kept in mind that Article 122 of the Qanune-Shahadat, 1984 comes into play only when the prosecution has proved the guilt of the accused by producing sufficient evidence, except the facts referred in Article 122 Qanun- e-Shahadat, 1984, leading to the inescapable conclusion that the offence was committed by the accused.

Article 122 reads as under: “122. Burden of proving fact especially within knowledge:- When any fact is especially within the knowledge of any person, the burden to proving that fact is upon him.” It has to be kept in mind that Article 122 of the Qanune-Shahadat, 1984 comes into play only when the prosecution has proved the guilt of the accused by producing sufficient evidence, except the facts referred in Article 122 Qanun- e-Shahadat, 1984, leading to the inescapable conclusion that the offence was committed by the accused. Then, the burden is on the accused not to prove his innocence, but only to produce evidence enough to create doubts in the prosecution's case. It may be noted that this issue was also dilated upon by the august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case of “Rehmat alias Rahman alias Waryam alias Badshah v. The State” (PLD 1977 SC 515), where, while deliberating upon Section 106 of the Evidence Act, which is para materia with Article 122 of the Qanune-Shahadat, 1984, it held as under: “Needless to emphasis that in spite of section 106 of the Evidence Act in criminal case the onus rests on the prosecution to prove the guilt of the accused beyond reasonable doubt and this section cannot be construed to mean that the onus at any stage shifts on to the accused to prove his innocence or make up for the inability and failure of the prosecution to produce evidence to establish the guilt of the accused. Nor does it relieve! the prosecution of the burden to bring the guilt home to the accused. It is only after the prosecution has on the evidence adduced by it, succeeded in raising reasonable inference of the guilt of the accused, unless the same is rebutted, that this section wherever applicable, comes into play and the accused may negative the inference by proof of some facts within his special knowledge. If, however, the prosecution fails to prove the essential ingredients of the offence, no duty is cast on the accused to prove his innocence." 35 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 The ratio decidendi of the above decision was further developed by in the case of “NASRULLAH alias NASRO Versus The STATE (2017 S C M R 724), wherein, it held as under: “It has been argued by the learned counsel for the complainant that in the cases of Arshad Mehmood v.

If the article was to be interpreted otherwise, it would lead to the very startling conclusion that in a murder case the burden lies on the accused to prove that he/she did not commit the murder because who could 36 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 know better than he/she whether he/she did or did not. Article 122 of the Qanune-Shahadat, 1984 cannot be used to undermine the well-established rule of law that, save in a very exceptional class of cases, the burden is on the prosecution and never shifts. Throughout the web of the Law, one golden thread is always to be seen, that it is the duty of the prosecution to prove the accused's guilt subject to any statutory exception. No matter what the charge, the principle that the prosecution must prove the guilt of the accused is the law and no attempt to whittle it down can be entertained. The prosecution witnesses failed to provide evidence enabling us to determine the truthfulness of the motive alleged, and the fact that the said motive was so compelling that it could have led the appellants to have committed the Qatl-i- Amd of the deceased. There is a poignant hush with regard to the particulars of the motive alleged. No independent witness was produced by the prosecution to prove the motive as alleged. Even otherwise a tainted piece of evidence cannot corroborate another tainted piece of evidence. The august 42 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 Supreme Court of Pakistan has held in the case of Muhammad Javed v. The State (2016 SCMR 2021) as under: “The said related and chance witnesses had failed to receive any independent corroboration inasmuch as no independent proof of the motive set up by the prosecution had been brought on the record of the case.” 25. The only other piece of evidence left to be considered by us is the medical evidence with regard to the injuries observed on the dead body of the deceased by Dr. Mobeen Ahmad (PW-7) but the same is of no assistance in this case as medical evidence by its nature and character, cannot recognize a culprit in case of an unobserved incidence. The State” (2017 SCMR 986) has enunciated the following principle of law: “The medical evidence is only confirmatory or of supporting nature and is never held to be corroboratory evidence, to identify the culprit.” The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in its binding judgment titled “Naveed Asghar and two others Vs. The State” (P L D 2021 Supreme Court 600) has enunciated the following principle of law: “31. The prosecution has attempted to complete the chain of circumstantial evidence by medical evidence relating to the post mortem examinations of the deceased persons. This evidence proves only the factum that death of the deceased persons was caused by cutting their throats through some sharp edge weapon; it does in no way indicate who had cut their throats and with what particular weapon. Medical evidence is in the nature of supporting, confirmatory or explanatory of the direct or circumstantial evidence, and is not 43 Murder Reference No.02 of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 59-J of 2019 Criminal Appeal No. 61-J of 2019 "corroborative evidence" in the sense the term is used in legal parlance for a piece of evidence that itself also has some probative force to connect the accused person with the commission of offence. Medical evidence by itself does not throw any light on the identity of the offender. Such evidence may confirm the available substantive evidence with regard to certain facts including seat of the injury, nature of the injury, cause of the death, kind of the weapon used in the occurrence, duration between the injuries and the death, and presence of an injured witness or the injured accused at the place of occurrence, but it does not connect the accused with the commission of the offence.

It cannot constitute corroboration for proving involvement of the accused person in the commission of offence, as it does not establish the identity of the accused person.32 Therefore, the medical evidence is of little help to the prosecution for bringing home the guilt to the petitioners.” 26. Considering all the above circumstances, we entertain serious doubt in our minds regarding the involvement of the appellants namely Muhammad Imran son of Abdul Salam and Mst. Sajida Bibi wife of Maqbool Ahmad in the present case. It is a settled principle of law that for giving the benefit of the doubt it is not necessary that there should be so many circumstances rather if only a single circumstance creating reasonable doubt in the mind of a prudent person is available then such benefit is to be extended to an accused not as a matter of concession but as of right. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case of “Muhammad Mansha Vs. The State” (2018 SCMR 772) has enunciated the following principle: “Needless to mention that while giving the benefit of doubt to an accused it is not necessary that there should be many circumstances creating doubt. If there is a circumstance which creates reasonable doubt in a prudent mind about the guilt of the accused, then the accused would be entitled to the benefit of such doubt, not as a matter of grace and concession, but as a matter of right. It is based on the maxim, "it is better that ten guilty persons be acquitted rather than one innocent person be convicted". Reliance in this behalf can be made upon the cases of Tariq Pervez v. Mere heinousness of the offence if not proved to the hilt is not a ground to avail the majesty of the court to do complete justice. This is an established principle of law and equity that it is better that 100 guilty persons should let off but one innocent person should not suffer. As the preeminent English jurist William Blackstone wrote, "Better that ten guilty persons escape, than that one innocent suffer." All the contradictions noted by the learned High Court are sufficient to cast a shadow of doubt on the prosecution's case, which entitles the petitioner to the right of benefit of the doubt. It is a well settled principle of law that for the accused to be afforded this right of the benefit of the doubt it is not necessary that there should be many circumstances creating uncertainty and if there is only one doubt, the benefit of the same must got to the petitioner.
